findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AC4 migration-completeness grep to also cover the MagicMock `.return_value` assignment pattern (e.g. add `| grep -E 'AcliClient\.return_value|AcliClient\s*='` or enumerate all three injection shapes explicitly) so files using `fake_acli_mod.AcliClient.return_value = client` are caught.
evidence: AC4 states migration is COMPLETE when `grep -rn 'AcliClient\s*=\|_FakeAcliModule' tests/ | grep -i acli` returns zero hits at the _load_acli seam doubles
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_applier_assignee_soft_fail.py:165: `fake_acli_mod.AcliClient.return_value = fake_client` — MagicMock .return_value assignment, not matched by `AcliClient\s*=`
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_transition_error_soft_fail.py:136: same pattern
At least 8 files use `fake.AcliClient.return_value = client` or `fake_acli_mod.AcliClient.return_value = fake_client` — none matched by the AC4 grep
findings.finding: AC4's migration completeness grep (`'AcliClient\s*=\|_FakeAcliModule'`) misses the MagicMock `.return_value` assignment pattern — `fake_acli_mod.AcliClient.return_value = fake_client` — used in at least 8 applier test files (test_transition_error_soft_fail.py, test_applier_comment_error_surfaced.py, test_applier_stale_binding_404.py, test_applier_assignee_soft_fail.py, test_silent_noop_canary.py, test_applier_cross_project_guard.py, test_applier_create_comment_error_surfaced.py, test_fail_loud_comment_errors.py). These are module-shaped doubles that the gate is intended to catch. The grep could return zero hits while those doubles remain module-shaped, giving a false green on migration completeness.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4 — grep clause `grep -rn 'AcliClient\s*=\|_FakeAcliModule' tests/ | grep -i acli`
scenarios: Implementer migrates all `AcliClient = ...` and `_FakeAcliModule` occurrences, runs AC4 grep, gets zero hits and declares completion — but 8 test files still inject a module-shaped double via `.return_value =`, meaning those tests still construct a client via `acli_mod.AcliClient(...)` rather than using the transport directly.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC4 grep with: `grep -rn 'AcliClient\(\.return_value\)\?\s*=' tests/ | grep -i acli` — or more precisely, run two greps: the existing one plus `grep -rn 'AcliClient\.return_value' tests/` and assert both return zero hits in scope.

findings.finding: The plan lists `_attestation.py:82` as a transport/construction site requiring rewire (Scope + Approach) but provides no dedicated AC, no seam test description, and no decision on whether a new seam or a direct injection point is introduced — unlike `run_differs.py` which explicitly calls for a new seam + new test. An executor cannot determine whether a test is expected for `_attestation.py`'s rewire or whether it is considered covered by AC1's neutrality grep alone.
findings.location: Approach — '_attestation.py:82' paragraph; Scope bullet 1; Acceptance Criteria (all ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `grep -rn 'AcliClient\s*=\|_FakeAcliModule'` AC4 gate with a portable ERE form — e.g. `grep -rEn 'AcliClient[[:space:]]*=|_FakeAcliModule' tests/` — so the neutrality check produces correct results on both BSD grep (macOS) and GNU grep (Linux CI).
evidence: Plan AC4 verbatim: "grep -rn 'AcliClient\s*=\|_FakeAcliModule' tests/ | grep -i acli returns zero hits at the _load_acli seam doubles"
GNU grep `\|` alternation is not portable to BSD grep (macOS ships BSD grep); the equivalent portable form is `-E 'AcliClient[[:space:]]*=|_FakeAcliModule'`
macOS is a primary developer-workstation platform in the supported matrix
findings.finding: The AC4 neutrality-gate grep command uses GNU-grep alternation syntax (`\|`) that is not supported by BSD grep (macOS, FreeBSD). On macOS the command silently produces no output regardless of matches, making the gate pass falsely (zero hits even when seam doubles still expose `AcliClient` factories).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4: `grep -rn 'AcliClient\s*=\|_FakeAcliModule' tests/ | grep -i acli` as the migration-completeness verification gate
scenarios: macOS developer workstation running `make test` or the neutrality gate manually: BSD grep silently returns exit 0 with no output for the `\|` alternation, so the gate reports zero hits even when un-migrated `AcliClient`-factory doubles remain in the test suite — a false-green completeness check that allows the story to be closed with the migration incomplete.
findings.suggested_fix: grep -rEn 'AcliClient[[:space:]]*=|_FakeAcliModule' tests/ | grep -i acli

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the description of `acli_subprocess.py` in the `apply_handlers.py:181` fix section: it is a root reconciler module (`rebar_reconciler/acli_subprocess.py`), not an 'adapter-internal module' — this label is refuted by the file's confirmed location.
evidence: Plan: 'import the class directly — `from rebar_reconciler.acli_subprocess import AssigneeNotFoundError` (defined at `acli_subprocess.py:147`, an adapter-internal module)'
`acli_subprocess.py` is at `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_subprocess.py` (root of rebar_reconciler, not under `adapters/`)
`AssigneeNotFoundError` confirmed at `acli_subprocess.py:147`
findings.finding: The plan states `acli_subprocess.py` is 'an adapter-internal module' when prescribing `from rebar_reconciler.acli_subprocess import AssigneeNotFoundError`. In the current codebase `acli_subprocess.py` lives at `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_subprocess.py` — at the root of the reconciler package, not under `adapters/jira/`. It is not adapter-internal; it is a core sibling module. This matters because the plan's own out-of-scope rule says physical file relocation is S5, so if `acli_subprocess.py` is expected to be adapter-internal at this story's point of execution, the plan contradicts itself.
findings.location: Approach — Exception-class catch site (`apply_handlers.py:181`) subsection
scenarios: Minor: calling a root module 'adapter-internal' does not block implementation since the import path is correct. However, it signals a false premise about module location that could cause confusion during S5 relocation planning.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'an adapter-internal module' to 'a reconciler-root module (to be moved to the adapter in S5)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's description of `outbound_links._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`: `acli_graph.py` has NO `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` definition (grep-verified zero hits), so this story must CREATE the canonical copy under `adapters/jira/` and update `outbound_links.py` to import from it — not merely 'relocate from acli_graph'.
evidence: Plan: 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` ("keep in sync with acli_graph")'
grep of `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_graph.py` for `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` returns zero hits
`outbound_links.py:42` has the only definition of `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` in the repo
The AC says to single-source this into `adapters/jira/` — but the 'original' to single-source from does not exist
findings.finding: The plan asserts `outbound_links.py:30-54` holds 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`', implying a canonical definition already exists in `acli_graph.py` that S4 will single-source from. Grep of `acli_graph.py` returns zero hits for `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`. The symbol does not exist there — the comment in `outbound_links.py:30` says 'canonical definition lives in acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Cycle 1)' but this refers to a past state; the canonical copy was apparently never committed or was removed. There is no existing canonical definition to delegate to in `adapters/jira/`.
findings.location: Context / Confirmed leak sites — `outbound_links.py:30-54`
scenarios: The plan's prescription to 'single-source the canonical constant from `acli_graph`' into `adapters/jira/` cannot be executed as written — there is no canonical copy to extract; instead, a NEW canonical definition must be created in `adapters/jira/` and the `outbound_links.py` copy replaced.
An implementer who reads the plan literally will look for `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` to migrate, find nothing, and have no clear path forward.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`' with 'the only definition of `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` in the codebase; this story creates the canonical copy under `adapters/jira/` and replaces the inline definition in `outbound_links.py` with an import from the adapter.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's description of the `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` leak: `acli_graph.py` contains **no** `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` symbol (grep-confirmed); the constant's sole definition is in `outbound_links.py:42`; the task is migrating that sole definition into `adapters/jira/`, not de-duplicating against a non-existent canonical in `acli_graph`.
evidence: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/outbound_links.py:30: '# acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Cycle 1), but the differ is loaded'
src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/outbound_links.py:42: '_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK: dict[str, tuple[str, bool]] = {' — sole definition
grep of acli_graph.py for '_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK' returns zero matches — no canonical definition exists there
findings.finding: The plan asserts `outbound_links.py:30-54` holds 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`', implying a canonical definition exists in `acli_graph.py` that the plan will single-source by moving it to `adapters/jira/`. Grep of `acli_graph.py` returns zero matches for `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`. The constant exists only in `outbound_links.py` itself; `acli_graph.py` has no such symbol. The 'single-source' relocation is therefore not a de-duplication — it is a migration of the sole definition. The AC ('grepping the core sites for those constructs returns zero hits') is still achievable, but the executor will be confused by the 'keep in sync with acli_graph' framing when there is nothing in acli_graph to sync from.
findings.location: Context / Problem section — 'outbound_links.py:30-54 — a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK'
scenarios: Executor searches acli_graph.py for the 'canonical' constant to redirect outbound_links.py to, finds nothing, and either creates a duplicate or misunderstands the relocation task.
AC2 ('single-sourced under adapters/jira/') is achievable but the plan's rationale misdirects the executor about where the current duplicate lives.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK ("keep in sync with acli_graph")' with 'the sole definition of `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` (currently in `outbound_links.py:42`; the "keep in sync with acli_graph" comment is a historical note referencing a symbol that was never actually exported from `acli_graph.py`); the task is to move this definition into `adapters/jira/` so core no longer owns the link vocabulary'.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `outbound_links.py:30-54` holds 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`', implying `acli_graph.py` is the canonical source that already defines this constant. Grep of `acli_graph.py` for 'RELATION' (and 'JIRA_LINK') returns zero matches — `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` does not exist in `acli_graph.py`. `outbound_links.py` is the ONLY definition. The plan's deduplication framing ('single-source the duplicate into adapters/jira/') is correct in spirit but incorrect in premise: there is no pre-existing canonical copy to consolidate from; the plan is migrating the sole copy to a new home.
findings.location: Context section, 'Confirmed leak sites', outbound_links.py:30-54 bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`' with 'the sole definition of `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`, currently inlined in `outbound_links.py` with a stale comment saying "Keep in sync with acli_graph" — relocate it to `adapters/jira/` and import from there.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts `outbound_links.py:30-54` holds 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`', implying a canonical definition exists in `acli_graph.py`. Grepping `acli_graph.py` returns zero hits for `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` — the symbol does not exist there. The single definition is in `outbound_links.py` itself, which only comments 'keep in sync with acli_graph'. There is no duplicate; there is one definition and a stale comment.
findings.location: Context section — 'Confirmed leak sites', outbound_links.py:30-54 bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the bullet with: '`outbound_links.py:30-54` — defines `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` with a stale comment claiming a canonical source in `acli_graph.py` that no longer exists; relocate the single definition into `adapters/jira/` and delete the comment.'

findings.finding: The plan describes `outbound_links.py`'s `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` as 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`' that this story will single-source by moving the canonical to `adapters/jira/`. However, `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` does **not exist** in `acli_graph.py` — grep returns zero matches. The comment in `outbound_links.py:30` references it as the canonical location, but it was never defined there (or was removed). The constant in `outbound_links.py` is the only definition in the codebase. An executor expecting to find a canonical in `acli_graph` to reconcile against will find nothing and may introduce a wrong merge or create an unnecessary double-definition.
findings.location: Context section — 'Confirmed leak sites' / outbound_links.py:30-54; AC bullet 2
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`' with 'the only definition of `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` (the `acli_graph` canonical referenced in `outbound_links.py:30` does not exist — move this definition to `adapters/jira/` and delete it from `outbound_links.py`)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC#3 to distinguish the two uses of `"jira"` in `apply_inbound_records.py`: the provider/identity literals at :70/:74 (legitimately replaceable with `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention`) from the creation-channel literal at :156 (`validate_creation_channel("jira")`), which names a closed vocabulary value and should NOT be replaced with `IdentityConvention` — confirm whether :156 is in or out of scope for this story.
evidence: Plan scope: 'Replace the apply_inbound_records.py "jira" literals with RemoteRef/IdentityConvention'
AC#3: 'apply_inbound_records.py uses RemoteRef/IdentityConvention with no hardcoded "jira" provider/creation-channel literal'

src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_inbound_records.py:154-156: `from rebar.reducer._version import validate_creation_channel` … `create_data["creation_channel"] = validate_creation_channel("jira")`
src/rebar/reducer/_version.py:160: `CREATION_CHANNELS = frozenset({"cli", "mcp", "python", "jira", "import", "unknown"})` — 'jira' is a closed-vocabulary enum, not an identity convention
IdentityConvention (S2) is specified as the rebar-id label read/write convention over RemoteRef — semantically orthogonal to creation_channel
findings.finding: The plan directs replacing `apply_inbound_records.py`'s `"jira"` literals with `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention`, but the call at :156 — `validate_creation_channel("jira")` — passes `"jira"` as a closed-vocabulary creation-channel enum value validated against `CREATION_CHANNELS = frozenset({"cli", "mcp", "python", "jira", "import", "unknown"})`. `IdentityConvention` is a Protocol for rebar-id label stamping/parsing and `RemoteRef` is a remote-identity dataclass; neither semantically maps to a creation-channel argument. Substituting them here forces the new port abstractions onto a call site where `validate_creation_channel` is already the correct, purpose-built contract — cargo-cult application of the new abstractions beyond their semantic scope.
findings.location: Scope section / AC#3 / apply_inbound_records.py:156
scenarios: Executor substitutes IdentityConvention.vendor_name or similar into validate_creation_channel(), which is a string enum validator — the types are incompatible and the existing vocabulary contract is silently bypassed.

The 'jira' literal at :70/:74 (ensure_identity_for provider) is legitimately provider-identity and may be correctly replaced; but :156 is not — conflating the two leads to a wrong abstraction at the channel site.
findings.suggested_fix: Narrow AC#3 to: 'the hardcoded `"jira"` provider/identity literals at :70/:74 are replaced by `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention`; the `validate_creation_channel("jira")` call at :156 is out of scope (it uses a closed channel-vocabulary string, not an identity convention) and is verified untouched by a grep on the AC.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC#5) requiring a new or updated test covering the `_attestation.py` transport-injection seam, parallel to the new-test requirement already stated for `run_differs.py`.
evidence: Approach: 'For `_attestation`, route `build_acli_client_from_env` through the backend transport likewise.'
AC#5: explicitly requires 'a **new** test that patches it' for run_differs.py — no equivalent exists for _attestation.py
AC#1: only asserts 'no direct `AcliClient(` construction remains outside `adapters/jira/`' via grep — does not require test coverage of any new seam
findings.finding: `_attestation.py:82` is described in Approach as having 'no `_load_acli` seam' (same situation as `run_differs.py`) and requiring a new seam/transport-injection point, but — unlike `run_differs.py` — no AC requires a new test covering the new `_attestation.py` seam. AC#1's neutrality grep verifies removal of `AcliClient(` construction, but no criterion verifies the new seam is exercised by a test.
findings.location: Approach section ('`_attestation.py:82` — via `build_acli_client_from_env`… route through the backend transport') vs. AC#5 (covers only `run_differs.py`) vs. AC#1 (grep-only, no test-coverage requirement for _attestation)
scenarios: _attestation.py is rewired but its new seam has no test, leaving the construction path untested and potentially regressing silently if the transport injection is later broken.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC#5 to read: '…verified for `run_differs.py` by that new test passing under `make test` and by the AcliClient grep; **and** `_attestation.py`'s `build_acli_client_from_env` is covered by a corresponding new or updated test patching its new seam.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Scope section's hard 35-file count with the Approach's three-style enumeration and AC#4's property-based stance: either remove the count from Scope and rely solely on AC#4's grep property, or expand the count to include direct-assignment files.
evidence: Scope: 'Update the `_load_acli` seam tests in lockstep with the seam contract change (all **35** files — 30 `patch.object` + 5 `monkeypatch.setattr`)'
Approach: 'direct attribute assignment (`mod._load_acli = lambda: …` with save/restore — e.g. `conflict/test_parent_sync.py`, `mutate/test_comment_enrichment.py`, `mutate/test_link_sync.py`)'
AC#4: '(No hard file-count assertion — the count is fragile due to the two excluded self-defining `def _load_acli()` files and cross-style overlap.)'
findings.finding: The Scope section asserts exactly 35 files ("all **35** files — 30 `patch.object` + 5 `monkeypatch.setattr`") for the `_load_acli` seam migration, but the Approach section introduces a third injection style — direct attribute assignment (e.g., `conflict/test_parent_sync.py`, `mutate/test_comment_enrichment.py`, `mutate/test_link_sync.py`) — and AC#4 explicitly drops any file-count assertion as "fragile." An implementer reading Scope gets a hard target of 35 files; an implementer reading Approach+AC gets a property-based target with no count. These are contradictory signals about the migration boundary.
findings.location: Scope section ('Update the `_load_acli` seam tests … all **35** files — 30 patch.object + 5 monkeypatch.setattr') vs. Approach ('direct attribute assignment … `conflict/test_parent_sync.py`…') vs. AC#4 ('No hard file-count assertion')
scenarios: An implementer following the Scope section targets exactly 35 files and misses the direct-assignment style files, leaving those doubles module-shaped and the AC#4 property check failing.
An implementer following AC#4 ignores the Scope count entirely and is left without guidance on the expected scope of the migration.
findings.suggested_fix: Delete the '(all **35** files — 30 `patch.object` + 5 `monkeypatch.setattr`)' parenthetical from Scope and replace with '(all in-scope doubles across all three injection styles — see Approach for enumeration and AC#4 for the migration-complete property)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the fixed count '30 `patch.object` + 5 `monkeypatch.setattr` = 35 files' with the actual set: run `grep -rn 'patch.object.*_load_acli\|monkeypatch.setattr.*_load_acli\|\._load_acli\s*=' tests/` on current `main` and record the exact file list — grep finds substantially more than 35 injection sites.
evidence: Plan: 'Update the `_load_acli` seam tests in lockstep with the seam contract change (all **35** files — 30 `patch.object` + 5 `monkeypatch.setattr`)'

Grep of `tests/` for `_load_acli` finds: `patch.object` invocations in test_applier.py (8×), test_applier_rebase_retry.py (3×), test_applier_mapping.py (3×), test_applier_provenance.py (6×), test_applier_drift.py (4×), test_applier_stale_binding_404.py (2×), test_applier_assignee_soft_fail.py (2×), test_applier_reschedule.py (5×), test_applier_cross_project_guard.py (2×), test_applier_comment_error_surfaced.py (2×), test_applier_create_comment_error_surfaced.py (2×), test_fail_loud_comment_errors.py (2×), test_applier_rebase_retry.py, test_silent_noop_canary.py, test_transition_error_soft_fail.py (2×), test_bridge_alerts_surface.py (2×), test_fetcher_snapshot.py (7×), test_fetcher_dedup_observable.py, test_fetcher_truncation_gate.py (3×), test_fetcher_split_jql_queries.py (3×), test_fetcher_project_scoped_jql.py, test_fetcher_pagination_jql.py (2×), test_fetcher_unmapped_status.py (3×), test_reconcile_once.py (10×), test_e2e_dedup_pass.py, test_apply_batch_drift_tolerance.py (4×) — grossly more than 30
The plan itself notes the count is 'fragile' but still asserts '35' in Scope
findings.finding: The Scope section asserts 'all **35** files — 30 `patch.object` + 5 `monkeypatch.setattr`' for the `_load_acli` seam migration. A grep of the test tree finds approximately 47+ `patch.object` invocations of `_load_acli` across at least 20+ distinct test files — far more than 30. The `monkeypatch.setattr` style is used in `test_conflict_filing_deferred.py` (2×), `test_hard_delete_recreate.py` (1×), `test_inbound_leaf_bodies.py` (1×), `test_reconcile_idempotency.py` (2×), and `jira_contract/_fakes.py` (1×). Direct attribute assignment appears in `test_parent_sync.py`, `test_comment_enrichment.py`, `test_link_sync.py`. The total substantially exceeds 35.
findings.location: Scope — '_load_acli seam tests in lockstep (all **35** files — 30 `patch.object` + 5 `monkeypatch.setattr`)'
scenarios: Implementers treating '35' as a checklist bound will miss files, leaving module-shaped doubles that expose AcliClient factories — the very thing AC#4's grep is intended to catch.
The neutrality-check grep at AC#4 would catch missed files, but only after they are discovered at test-run time.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the '35' hard-count from Scope and replace it with 'all files matched by `grep -rn \'patch\.object.*_load_acli\|monkeypatch\.setattr.*_load_acli\|\._load_acli\s*=\' tests/` excluding the two self-defining `def _load_acli()` files'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Scope bullet and AC to update ADR §(a)'s confirmed-leak-site list to match the grep-verified set (remove `baseline_shadow.py`, add `apply_inbound.py`) before this story closes.
evidence: "the ADR §(a) list is stale — it names `baseline_shadow.py`, which does not exist, and omits `apply_inbound.py`"
ABSENCE: no Scope bullet or AC item says 'update ADR §(a) to reflect verified leak sites'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly calls out that the ADR §(a) list is stale — it names a non-existent file (`baseline_shadow.py`) and omits `apply_inbound.py` — but neither the Scope nor the ACs include a task to correct this. The stale reference will persist in the project's authoritative ADR after this story lands.
findings.location: Context / Problem section — 'the ADR §(a) list is stale — it names `baseline_shadow.py`, which does not exist, and omits `apply_inbound.py`'
scenarios: A future developer consults the ADR to enumerate leak sites, finds `baseline_shadow.py` listed, wastes time searching for a file that doesn't exist.
A subsequent story (e.g. S5) scoped by the ADR list silently excludes `apply_inbound.py`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Update ADR §(a) confirmed-leak-site list to the grep-verified set.' Add a one-line AC: 'ADR §(a) names exactly the 12 rewired modules and no non-existent files — verified by `test -f` for each listed path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one test (unit or integration) that calls the *real* `_load_acli()` implementation — without replacing it at the seam — against a configured `Backend` (even the existing Jira adapter stub), verifying `_load_acli()` returns a `TicketTransport`-shaped object, so the new seam body is exercised end-to-end.
evidence: 'Every test that injects at the `_load_acli` seam is in scope, across three injection styles: patch.object, monkeypatch.setattr, and direct attribute assignment' — all three styles replace the seam entirely, bypassing the new body

'post-change, `_load_acli()` returns the selected backend's TicketTransport object directly (i.e. `select_backend(config).transport`)' — this is the new implementation body that is never called in tests
No AC or Testing item exercises `_load_acli()` as-implemented (the real body) against a configured backend, even a stub one
S3 tests `select_backend` but not `select_backend(config).transport` as composed inside `_load_acli()`
findings.finding: All 35 `_load_acli` seam tests short-circuit the seam's new implementation body by replacing `_load_acli` wholesale, so no test exercises the actual `select_backend(config).transport` call that `_load_acli()` will now contain. If `select_backend` returns an object whose `.transport` attribute is missing, mistyped, or uses wrong config keys in this context, every test passes green while production `_load_acli()` calls fail at runtime.
findings.location: Approach section — '_load_acli seam' + Testing/AC sections
scenarios: Config key mismatch causes `select_backend(config)` to return a backend with no `.transport`
`select_backend` returns the right type but `.transport` is `None` due to lazy init — all seam-patched tests pass, live path raises `AttributeError`
New backend type added later; `_load_acli()`'s body silently returns wrong transport; seam tests never detect it
findings.suggested_fix: In the integration test module (or a new `test_load_acli_seam.py`), configure a minimal in-process `Backend` (using the existing Jira adapter test config), call `applier._load_acli()` / `fetcher._load_acli()` directly without patching, and assert the return value satisfies the `TicketTransport` interface (e.g., `isinstance(result, TicketTransport)` or `hasattr(result, 'search_issues')`). This is the one test that must call the real body. Keep all other tests seam-patched as planned.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the `_load_acli` seam contract change — e.g., describe an expand-contract phase (temporarily support both module and transport return shapes, then remove the module shape), a named git strategy (single atomic commit vs. stacked PRs with revert tags), or an explicit 'revert by reverting commit X' instruction.
evidence: Approach: 'New return-value contract (pinned): post-change, `_load_acli()` returns the selected backend's **`TicketTransport` object directly** … not the acli module. Call sites change from `acli_mod.AcliClient(jira_url=…)` to using the returned transport directly'
No expand-contract step described (e.g., a transitional `_load_acli` that supports both return shapes)
No rollback path described (e.g., a feature flag, a revert strategy, or a branch strategy)
findings.finding: The `_load_acli` return-type change is a breaking interface change affecting ~35 test doubles simultaneously. The plan acknowledges the breakage for `apply_handlers.py` and the individual seam sites, but provides no rollback path or expand-contract sequencing for the seam contract itself. If the migration is interrupted (e.g., a CI failure midway through the double migration), the codebase is left in a mixed state where some doubles are transport-shaped and others are module-shaped, with no described mechanism to revert.
findings.location: Approach section ('New return-value contract (pinned): post-change, `_load_acli()` returns the selected backend's **`TicketTransport` object directly`') and Testing section
scenarios: CI fails mid-migration (e.g., after applier/fetcher are migrated but before run_differs and _attestation are done); the codebase has mixed double shapes and the seam contract is ambiguous.
A post-merge regression in a downstream consumer requires reverting the story; there is no guidance on what a clean revert looks like.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the `_load_acli` seam's new return-type contract (returns `TicketTransport` directly, not the acli module) in the existing Backend-port ADR (S2 doc) or a brief AGENTS.md note, and reference it from this story's Scope/ACs.
evidence: "post-change, `_load_acli()` returns the selected backend's `TicketTransport` object directly (i.e. `select_backend(config).transport`), *not* the acli module"
ABSENCE: no mention of writing an ADR update, adding to AGENTS.md, or amending S2's interface doc to record this seam contract
The plan references S2's ADR as the existing doc for the Backend port but does not say this story will append to or amend it.
findings.finding: The `_load_acli` seam contract change — from returning an acli *module* to returning a bare `TicketTransport` object — is an architectural decision that affects ~35 test files and every downstream consumer of the seam, but the plan includes no ADR or design-doc update to capture this new contract.
findings.location: Approach section — '_load_acli() returns the selected backend's TicketTransport object directly' and Scope — 'Update the _load_acli seam tests in lockstep'
scenarios: A developer adding a new transport site in a future story consults the seam docs, finds only the old module-return contract, and wires the site incorrectly.
A reviewer of S5 (physical relocation) is unaware that `_load_acli` now returns a transport, not a module, and interprets test doubles incorrectly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either promote `AssigneeNotFoundError` to the `Backend`/`TicketTransport` port layer (or a shared `exceptions.py`) so `apply_handlers.py` imports from the port, not from `acli_subprocess` directly — or explicitly justify and document the temporary coupling with a TODO referencing S5.
evidence: "adapter-internal module" is the plan's own characterisation of `acli_subprocess`
The story's purpose is to stop core modules importing adapter internals; this fix introduces a new such import in the same story
ABSENCE: no mitigation is proposed (e.g. defining `AssigneeNotFoundError` on the `TicketTransport` port or in a shared exceptions module)
findings.finding: The plan routes `apply_handlers.py`'s `AssigneeNotFoundError` catch by importing the class *directly* from `acli_subprocess` — explicitly described as an 'adapter-internal module'. This creates a new coupling from a core module to an adapter-internal implementation detail, which is architecturally inconsistent with the isolation goal this story is building.
findings.location: Approach section — 'import the class directly — `from rebar_reconciler.acli_subprocess import AssigneeNotFoundError` (defined at `acli_subprocess.py:147`, an adapter-internal module)'
scenarios: S5 physically moves `acli_subprocess` into `adapters/jira/`; `apply_handlers.py` now has a broken import that must be patched again.
A second backend adapter is added; `apply_handlers.py` remains coupled to the Jira adapter's exception class, making the soft-fail path non-portable.
findings.suggested_fix: Define `AssigneeNotFoundError` (or a base `TransportError` it subclasses) in the port module introduced in S2, re-export it from `acli_subprocess` for backward compat, and have `apply_handlers.py` import from the port. If that is too large for this story's scope, add a `# TODO(S5): move to port` comment and an AC that S5 resolves the coupling.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `inbound_fields.py` is classified as a 'vendor module' subject to the AC#1 neutrality grep: if it stays at the root (S5 scope), the AC#1 gate must not ban `inbound_fields` imports in S4; if it must be hidden behind the Backend port in S4, explain the rewire path that does not physically move the file.
evidence: Plan: '`inbound_differ.py:141` — `_map_jira_to_local_fields(...)` (inbound mapper)'
`inbound_differ.py:31-41` imports `_map_jira_to_local_fields` from `rebar_reconciler.inbound_fields`
`inbound_fields.py` is at `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py` (root of rebar_reconciler, not under `adapters/`)
AC#1 neutrality grep lists `inbound_fields` as a 'root vendor module' to be eliminated from core imports
findings.finding: The plan classifies `inbound_differ.py:141` as a 'vendor mapper import leak' — one of the '8 core importer sites' to route through the Backend port. But `_map_jira_to_local_fields` is imported at `inbound_differ.py:40` from `rebar_reconciler.inbound_fields` (a root-level core module, not `adapters.jira.*` or any vendor module). `inbound_fields.py` is confirmed to live at `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py`, not under `adapters/`. The plan's neutrality gate (AC#1) tests that core modules do not import `adapters.jira.*` or 'the root vendor modules (`outbound_fields`/`inbound_fields`/`adf`/`comment_limits`/`acli*`)' — the gate explicitly lists `inbound_fields` as a vendor module to be excised, but the plan never explains why `inbound_fields.py` (a plain core module) is treated as a vendor module whose import constitutes a leak.
findings.location: Context / Confirmed leak sites — `inbound_differ.py:141`; Acceptance Criteria #1
scenarios: If `inbound_fields.py` is indeed meant to move to `adapters/jira/` as part of S5, then the S4 neutrality gate would prematurely fail until S5 lands — but the plan says S5 is out of scope.
If `inbound_fields.py` is NOT moving, then the AC#1 neutrality gate that bans core imports of `inbound_fields` cannot be satisfied within this story's scope — the gate would be permanently broken.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the edit-set the ~6 test files that set `fake_acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError = acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError` (including `test_transition_error_soft_fail.py`, `test_applier_assignee_soft_fail.py`, `test_applier_stale_binding_404.py`, `test_applier_comment_error_surfaced.py`, `test_silent_noop_canary.py`, `test_fail_loud_comment_errors.py`, `test_applier_create_comment_error_surfaced.py`) and clean up those now-dead attribute assignments when migrating fakes to transport-shaped doubles.
evidence: tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_transition_error_soft_fail.py:137,187: `fake_acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError = acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError`
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_applier_assignee_soft_fail.py:169,221: same pattern
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_applier_stale_binding_404.py:123: same pattern
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_applier_comment_error_surfaced.py:93: same pattern
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_silent_noop_canary.py:90: same pattern
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_fail_loud_comment_errors.py:104: same pattern
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_applier_create_comment_error_surfaced.py:93: same pattern
Plan edit-set for AssigneeNotFoundError fix: 'this story adds apply_handlers.py to its edit-set' — no test files named
findings.finding: The plan names `apply_handlers.py` as the sole file to update for the `AssigneeNotFoundError` catch fix, but at least 6 additional test files inject `AssigneeNotFoundError` onto their module-shaped `fake_acli_mod` objects (e.g. `fake_acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError = acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError`) specifically because `applier.py` used to access the exception class via the acli module. After `apply_handlers.py` is rewritten to catch via a direct import, these assignments become dead code in the test fakes — they are unenumerated consumers of the old `ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError` pattern. The plan's edit-set and AC#6 do not mention cleaning up these 6+ test files.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Exception-class catch site (apply_handlers.py:181)'; Acceptance Criteria AC#6
scenarios: Executor changes apply_handlers.py correctly but leaves 6+ test fake constructors setting `.AssigneeNotFoundError` on a transport-shaped double, producing dead assignments. The AC#6 grep ('ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError' returning zero hits) still passes, but the test fakes silently carry a vestigial attribute that future readers will misinterpret as part of the TicketTransport contract.
A future story that enforces the TicketTransport surface via a strict Protocol or dataclass finds unexpected extra attributes on all these fakes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale sentence to the Approach section explaining why all 12 sites must be rewired atomically in one story (e.g. because the neutrality gate is an all-or-nothing invariant, or because the `_load_acli` contract change must be consistent across all consumers to avoid test-seam confusion).
evidence: The plan explains why transport is returned directly vs. whole Backend, but gives no positive rationale for why ALL 12 sites are rewired atomically in one story vs. a per-module incremental strategy.
ABSENCE: no sentence of the form 'we do all sites in one story because...' — the only structural justification is about the `_load_acli` return type, not the scope of the atomic unit.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for the overall atomic-rewire approach (all 12 sites + 4 transport sites in one story, with all test doubles migrated simultaneously) vs. the structural alternative of per-module incremental migration behind a thin module-facade shim. The only stated rationale covers one sub-choice (transport vs. full Backend return value from `_load_acli()`), not the larger choice.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Picking this concrete shape (vs. returning a whole Backend and doing `_load_acli().transport` at call sites) is deliberate'
scenarios: An implementer wonders why they can't split this into per-module PRs (e.g. just outbound_differ first) — the plan doesn't explain the ordering/atomicity requirement that makes single-story all-sites necessary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (or add to S2's AC) that `Backend` / `JiraBackend` exposes a `.transport` attribute returning the `TicketTransport`, so `select_backend(config).transport` is a valid accessor when S4 lands.
evidence: Plan quote: '_load_acli() returns the selected backend's **TicketTransport object directly** (i.e. select_backend(config).transport)'

S2 ticket (ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798) defines Backend, five role Protocols including TicketTransport, and JiraBackend — but never specifies a `.transport` attribute on Backend or JiraBackend as part of the interface contract.
S3 ticket (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) describes select_backend(config) returning a Backend but does not specify a `.transport` accessor either.

The plan references select_backend(config).transport in multiple places (Approach, AC for run_differs.py, AC for applier/fetcher) but the attribute is nowhere confirmed as part of the S2 Backend interface.
findings.finding: The plan's seam-contract evolution relies on `select_backend(config).transport` to obtain the TicketTransport, but neither the S2 spec (ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798) nor the S3 spec (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) explicitly defines a `.transport` attribute on `Backend` or `JiraBackend`. If S2 lands without this accessor, every `_load_acli()` call site that does `select_backend(config).transport` will AttributeError at runtime, and the seam evolution collapses.
findings.location: Approach section — 'New return-value contract (pinned): post-change, _load_acli() returns the selected backend's **`TicketTransport` object directly** (i.e. `select_backend(config).transport`)'
scenarios: S2 is implemented with JiraBackend wrapping AcliClient but exposing no .transport property — S4 arrives and all _load_acli seam sites AttributeError on the .transport lookup.
S2 implements a different accessor name (e.g. .client or .acli_transport) — S4's grep-based AC check passes zero hits for AcliClient( but the transport lookup silently fails at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S2's AC (or the S4 plan's 'prerequisites verified' section): '`JiraBackend.transport: TicketTransport` is a defined attribute, verified by `assert hasattr(select_backend(load_config()), "transport")`.' Alternatively, specify this accessor name explicitly in the S4 plan so the implementor knows to add it to S2 if missing.

findings.finding: AC4's exact cardinality assertion (`grep -rl '_load_acli' tests/ | wc -l` == 35) conflicts with the plan's stated addition of a **new** test for `run_differs.py`'s new seam. If the new seam is named `_load_acli` the count becomes 36 and the AC fails despite being correct; if named differently (e.g. `_load_transport`), the new test is invisible to this grep and its coverage goes unverified — either way the assertion is wrong or incomplete.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4; Approach section ('add a fresh test that patches the new seam')
findings.suggested_fix: Split AC4 into two assertions: (1) `grep -rl '_load_acli' tests/ | wc -l` == 35 for the *existing* applier/fetcher seam files (unchanged count confirms none were dropped), plus (2) a separate check — e.g. `grep -rl '<new-seam-name>' tests/ | wc -l` == 1 — that confirms the new `run_differs` seam test exists and names its patch target explicitly.

findings.finding: AC#4 pins the post-story grep count at exactly `wc -l == 35`, but the Approach simultaneously calls for adding a brand-new test file for run_differs.py's new seam (which has no pre-existing `_load_acli` patch target). If that new test references a seam named `_load_acli`, the true file count becomes 36, making the equality assertion fail on a correct implementation. If named differently, the grep misses it and provides no coverage guarantee for the new seam.
findings.location: Approach (run_differs.py:403 paragraph) vs AC#4 (`grep -rl '_load_acli' tests/ | wc -l == 35`)
findings.suggested_fix: If the new seam in run_differs is named `_load_acli`, change AC#4 to `wc -l == 36`. If given a distinct name (e.g., `_load_transport`), add a second grep line in AC#4: `grep -rl '<new_seam_name>' tests/ | wc -l == 1` alongside the existing `== 35` check.

findings.finding: The plan states the 35 `_load_acli` test files break down as '30 via `patch.object(applier|fetcher, "_load_acli", …)` and 5 via `monkeypatch.setattr(…, "_load_acli", …)`' with both styles covered. Grep-verified counts show only ~25 files use `patch.object`, ~4 use `monkeypatch.setattr` directly, and a THIRD injection style — direct module attribute assignment (`fetcher_mod._load_acli = lambda: ...` / save-and-restore pattern) — appears in at least 3 test files (`test_parent_sync.py`, `test_comment_enrichment.py`, `test_link_sync.py`) that the plan's two-style taxonomy does not name or cover.
findings.location: Approach section, 'Exact test-double migration surface (grep-verified against current main)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: '(3) 3 files use bare direct assignment (`mod._load_acli = lambda: stub_module`) — test_parent_sync.py, test_comment_enrichment.py, test_link_sync.py — update each to inject a transport-shaped SimpleNamespace instead of a module-shaped stub.'

findings.finding: The plan categorizes `_load_acli` seam injection in tests as exactly '30 via `patch.object` + 5 via `monkeypatch.setattr`', pinning the post-migration grep-file count at 35. However, at least three test files (`tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/conflict/test_parent_sync.py`, `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/mutate/test_comment_enrichment.py`, `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/mutate/test_link_sync.py`) inject via direct attribute assignment (`fetcher_mod._load_acli = lambda: ...`) — a third injection style not enumerated. Additionally, two files (`tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/conflict/test_status_transition_rest.py`, `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/mutate/test_assignee_validation.py`) define a local `_load_acli()` function of their own (not seam-patching) and would appear in the `grep -rl` count, inflating it. The AC#4 cardinality gate (`wc -l == 35`) is therefore built on an incorrect taxonomy.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Exact test-double migration surface'; AC#4

findings.finding: `tests/integration/rebar_reconciler/jira_contract/_fakes.py` injects a module-shaped double (`_FakeAcliModule`) with an `AcliClient` factory via `monkeypatch.setattr(fetcher_mod, '_load_acli', lambda: _FakeAcliModule(...))`. After this story's seam change, `_load_acli()` returns a transport directly, not a module with `AcliClient`. This file is not named in the plan's 35-file seam-migration surface or anywhere in the scope/approach, yet it uses the seam and its `FakeAcliClient` implements the full fetcher method surface (`search_issues`, `get_comment_map`, `get_issuelinks_map`, `get_parent_map`) that the new transport shape may not expose.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Exact test-double migration surface (grep-verified)'; Scope section

findings.finding: The plan's stated `TicketTransport` double shape — `SimpleNamespace(create_issue=…, search_issues=…, transition_issue_by_name=…, add_label=…, add_comment=…)` — is asserted without verifying it covers the read methods (`get_comment_map`, `get_issuelinks_map`, `get_parent_map`) that the existing fetcher-side fakes (`_fakes.py::FakeAcliClient`) expose and that the fetcher uses. If `TicketTransport` omits these read methods, the 4+ integration tests that install `_FakeAcliModule` via `_fakes.install()` cannot be migrated to the uniform 'transport-shaped double' the plan prescribes — they need a separate read-surface, which the plan never names or accounts for.
findings.location: Approach section — 'New return-value contract (pinned)' / 'Exact test-double migration surface' paragraph; tests/integration/rebar_reconciler/jira_contract/_fakes.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add `get_comment_map`, `get_issuelinks_map`, `get_parent_map` to the listed `TicketTransport` surface in the 'New return-value contract (pinned)' paragraph, and update the example `SimpleNamespace` to include them. If the read surface is intentionally a separate Protocol (e.g. `SupportsRead`), name it explicitly and note which of the 35 test files use the write-only shape vs. the read+write shape.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
